theorem_statement:
Let \(X\) be a normed real vector space. Let \(K\subset X\) be nonempty, compact, and convex. If \(T:K\to K\) is continuous, then there exists \(x\in K\) such that

\[
T(x)=x.
\]
raw_proof_text:
We prove the theorem using Brouwer’s fixed-point theorem in finite dimensions.

Fix \(\varepsilon>0\). Since \(T(K)\subseteq K\) and \(K\) is compact, the set \(T(K)\) is compact. Therefore there exist finitely many points

\[
y_1,\ldots,y_m\in T(K)
\]

such that

\[
T(K)\subseteq \bigcup_{i=1}^m B(y_i,\varepsilon).
\]

We will construct a continuous finite-dimensional approximation to \(T\).

For (y\in T(K)), define

\[
\alpha_i(y)=\max\{0,\varepsilon-|y-y_i|\}.
\]

Since the balls (B(y_i,\varepsilon)) cover \(T(K)\), for every (y\in T(K)) at least one (\alpha_i(y)) is positive. Hence

\[
A(y)=\sum_{i=1}^m \alpha_i(y)>0.
\]

Define

\[
\lambda_i(y)=\frac{\alpha_i(y)}{A(y)}.
\]

Then each (\lambda_i(y)\ge 0), the functions \(\lambda_i\) are continuous, and

\[
\sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i(y)=1.
\]

Now define

\[
P(y)=\sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i(y)y_i.
\]

The point (P(y)) lies in the finite-dimensional convex hull

\[
C=\operatorname{conv}\{y_1,\ldots,y_m\}.
\]

Since each (y_i\in T(K)\subseteq K) and \(K\) is convex, we have

\[
C\subseteq K.
\]

Moreover, if (\lambda_i(y)>0), then \(|y-y_i|<\varepsilon\). Therefore

\[
|P(y)-y|
=

\left|\sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i(y)(y_i-y)\right|
\le
\sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i(y)|y_i-y|
<
\varepsilon.
\]

Thus \(P\) is a continuous map from \(T(K)\) into the finite-dimensional compact convex set \(C\), and it uniformly approximates the identity on \(T(K)\) within \(\varepsilon\).

Now consider the map

\[
F_\varepsilon:C\to C
\]

defined by

\[
F_\varepsilon(x)=P(T(x)).
\]

This is continuous because both \(T\) and \(P\) are continuous. The set \(C\) is a compact convex subset of the finite-dimensional vector space

\[
\operatorname{span}{y_1,\ldots,y_m}.
\]

By Brouwer’s fixed-point theorem, \(F_\varepsilon\) has a fixed point \(x_\varepsilon\in C\). Thus

\[
x_\varepsilon=P(T(x_\varepsilon)).
\]

Since \(P\) approximates the identity on \(T(K)\), we get

\[
|x_\varepsilon-T(x_\varepsilon)|
=

|P(T(x_\varepsilon))-T(x_\varepsilon)|
<
\varepsilon.
\]

So for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists \(x_\varepsilon\in K\) such that

\[
|x_\varepsilon-T(x_\varepsilon)|<\varepsilon.
\]

Now choose a sequence \(\varepsilon_n\to 0\), for instance \(\varepsilon_n=1/n\), and let \(x_n=x_{\varepsilon_n}\). Since \(K\) is compact, there is a subsequence \(x_{n_j}\) converging to some point \(x\in K\). We have

\[
|x_{n_j}-T(x_{n_j})|<\varepsilon_{n_j}\to 0.
\]

Since \(T\) is continuous,

\[
T(x_{n_j})\to T(x).
\]

But \(x_{n_j}\to x\), and the distance between \(x_{n_j}\) and \(T(x_{n_j})\) tends to zero. Therefore

\[
T(x)=x.
\]

Indeed,

\[
|x-T(x)|
\le
|x-x_{n_j}|
+
|x_{n_j}-T(x_{n_j})|
+
|T(x_{n_j})-T(x)|,
\]

and all three terms tend to zero as \(j\to\infty\).

Thus \(T\) has a fixed point in \(K\).
